speaker: interviewer
text: September the 8th, 1996. The survivor is Gunther Katz. Hilary Helstein, interviewer. It's in Los Angeles, California, USA. It'll be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's September 8, 1996. My name is Hilary Helstein. And I am interviewing Gunther Katz. We're conducting this interview in North Hollywood, California, in the United States of America, in the English language. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Can you please tell me your name-- your full name now, if your name at birth was anything different, and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Gunther B. Katz. At birth, I was Bernhard Gunter, spelled G-U umlaut-- N-T-E-R Katz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your birth date and the place you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on August 17, 1929. And I was born in Mannheim, Germany, and later lived my life in Europe in Germany, in Speyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's Speyer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Speyer is about 20 miles south of Mannheim on the Rhine. It's a famous old Jewish town that has existed since about the 11th century.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your childhood. Tell me about your parents. What were their names? What were their occupations?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Gertrude. She was called Trude. My father's name is Sally. My father was born in Guxhagen in northern Germany. And my mother was born in Speyer. They were married in-- on September 25, 1925, in Speyer. And we lived in Speyer the whole time. My mother was a homemaker. My father was a salesman. He was partner, and worked for my grandfather in a business that sold upholstery materials and saddle materials to upholsterers and saddlemakers throughout southern Germany. And traveled five days a week at doing that. He would come home Friday afternoon and leave on Monday morning. And my mother was always home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what your weekends were like then, when you got to see your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SOBBING] He always spent a lot of time with me when he was home. And it was probably the nicest time that I remember with him. He and I would take walks along the Rhine and spend a lot of time together. We were very observant, so on Shabbat, we always went to services, and then would come home for lunch. And then after that, since there was nothing else that you could do, we would take walks and do things that we could do on the Shabbat. He [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was a man that I admired greatly in many ways. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in one way, he was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very hesitant in that he never saw the danger that was coming, and always negated it as being, oh, nothing to worry about. Even when others were leaving, he insisted there was nothing to be worried about until nearly the very end, when it was too late. My mother was an excellent cook. She was a tremendous disciplinarian. Her most famous saying was, any [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ohrfeigen that she missed were wasted. And she would hit me at the least provocation when I did something wrong, whereas my father would always say, let the kid go, let the kid go. And so my mother was a disciplinarian and very strict with me, and a very smart woman in many, many ways, because at a very early age, she taught me things that would come handy much later on. I remember when I was six or seven years old, she taught me how to mend socks. She insisted that I knew the birth dates of all the relatives. When I was about eight, she started having me memorize addresses, knowing what was coming. And all these things were then in my head. And no matter what I lost, it wasn't until I got to Switzerland many years later that I wrote all these things down. And they were still as fluent as that. And as a result, I was able to get a hold of relatives and so forth. She did most of the cooking for our household. When I was little-- when I was born, we lived in a house separate from my grandparents. It was a house that my grandparents owned. And then in 1932, when I was about three years old, we moved out of that house for two reasons. Number one, the Germans had built a new bridge over the Rhine, and the access dam for the railroad came right in front of the house. And as a result, you couldn't see anything anymore. It was a very high dam. And the other reason was that my mother's youngest sister had just married and was moving away. And my grandparents felt that they didn't want to live alone. And the three of us moved in with them. And mother did most of the cooking, although my grandmother supervised everything and was an excellent cook also. They sort of managed the house together. And where we lived there was in a house. On the first floor was the business. And then the top two floors were living quarters. So my grandfather, my father were very close to the business at all times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your grandparents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's name was Elias Kling and my grandmother was Mina Kling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was a very easygoing man. I remember him best as going to work in the morning, coming home for lunch, and then wanting his nap. And nobody could breathe while he was taking his nap. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then, after his nap, he would go back to work-- came up at 4:00 and would expect a cup of coffee and a fresh roll on his plate. My grandmother was a very religious person, more so than my father-- my grandfather. She was totally Orthodox. And when she couldn't go to services, she would pray at home. She prayed mornings and evening in the living room. And when she prayed, we had to tippy-toe around the house. But other than that, she was a wonderful grandmother and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a very giving person for everyone. She was always the peacemaker in the family if anything went wrong. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Both of them were deported to Theresienstadt in July of 1942. And I've just recently found out through the Red Cross that they were then moved to southern Poland, to a camp. And my grandfather was seen working on a road gang by a neighbor from Speyer in 1943, in the spring. And this man spoke to him, and was sure that it was my grandfather. And then they-- there is no other record of them. The man told my cousin this after the war, when my cousin went to Speyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what holidays were like in your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were wonderful. I didn't like all the praying at home around the table. And my cousin and I would always do a lot of tomfoolery. If we sat opposite each other, we would start giggling at each other on Shabbat or a Seder, when-- so my mother decided we would sit with an adult between us on one side of the table so we couldn't see each other. But we still managed to look down and look down and start trouble. And then we would get disciplined. But in-- in the synagogue-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the synagogue was divided-- men downstairs, women upstairs. But the children had a separate bench over on the side, next to the men. And I was always opposite my father. And anytime my dad felt that I was either being impatient or that I was starting to start something, he would signal me, here. And I would have to stand next to him. He-- the synagogue was very interesting. It was Orthodox, but we had an organ. And I always remember the cantor had a button by his dais, and the organ was upstairs in the back. And whenever he wanted the organist to play something, he would push the button. And it would be-- run a-- ring a buzzer upstairs to start the organ. The rabbi later became our teacher when we couldn't go to school with the Germans anymore. And he became our teacher for all the classes. And the cantor would do some of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] religious school assignments for us. But the rabbi was the most dominant. The synagogue-- we had about 100 families in the-- in Speyer at that time when I was little. Then gradually, they dwindled [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as people left the country. And it got less and less. But in the beginning, we could fill the temple very nicely. The synagogue was about a 10-minute walk from our house. And of course, no cars in those days to go to temple. It was a walk in and a walk back. And on Yom Kippur, we would do it twice because we always took a break in the afternoon so that my grandfather and my grandmother could break fast for half-day fasting. And we would go home. And then usually, my father would stay the whole day. My father originally, when he was younger, wanted to become a rabbi. But he had no voice, same as I have no voice. And as a result, he became a very frustrated rabbi. And every now and then, when they needed a fill-in, he was always happy to fill in as a reader, or Torah reader, or whatever, and was very capable at it. The temple for us, in most cases, I would say, was always a happy experience-- although as a child, you look at it different. It's a pain to have to sit there for six hours or four hours. But later on, in retrospect, it was a good learning experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned schooling. Did you have any schooling with-- with German-- just regular German students--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --prior to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In '36, when I went into first grade, was the first year that the Germans had ruled that no Jewish kids could go into public school. So at that time, our synagogue had a room-- a meeting room upstairs that was then converted to become a classroom. And we had Rabbi Marx, who became our teacher. And we had all the classes in one room. And there was around 15 or 20 children all together in town at that time. And they were all the grades that you could imagine. And what he would do is he would do a little bit with one class and with another class. And in our-- in my class, the first grade class, there were four of us at the time. And there were some classes where there was only one student. And then there would be skipping a-- a grade, maybe. And there would be two or three in another grade. And he would teach at whatever grade level they were. And the teaching was mostly done around his desk because it was too difficult and too interruptive to do it throughout the room, with everybody else then having been disturbed because he would assign us other things to do. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The school was every day, Monday through Friday. And Wednesday afternoons, when normally we would not have school, because the German kids had Wednesday afternoons off, we would go to school. And we could, at that time, use the gym in the public school for the afternoon. So that's when we had our gymnastics. And that lasted a couple of years, and then that was cut off. But in the beginning, we would do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any contact with non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were-- there was one boy in particular who lived in our neighborhood, whom I was a good friend with until I was about six and a half years old. At that point-- we were the same age-- he joined the Hitler Youth and, overnight, became my enemy. He would never talk to me anymore. And any time I walked past his house-- which was all the time, because he lived on the way to the bakery-- he would taunt me and throw things at me, even that early. This was in 1936 and 1937. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on our way to school, all the children in our neighborhood would walk together so that we could protect each other from the taunting and the rock-throwing and whatever else that would go on, which was fairly constant. There would be days when nothing would happen. Then other days, a whole batch of them would come at us. And the-- the bad thing was that right down the street from where we lived was a public school that I should have gone to, and then directly across the back alley from where our synagogue was was another public school. So they would gang up on us there. And we would have to walk past the other one to go to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were two places where we had to pass where we would be recognized. Oh, the Jews are coming, the Jews are coming. So it was not an easy thing. And you always watched your back. Always.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't think I ever really got attacked. But I think the fear of the possibility, and the rocks and the words, were just as difficult for a six or seven-year-old as if they would have actually grabbed me and hit me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the things they said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jew, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Christ killer, and all these things that are normally said. And Jew, go away. Jew, get out. Jew-- Jew boy. And these kind of things. And a lot of these kids I knew by name because they were neighborhood kids. But they were not friends anymore. And where previously I could play on the street, it became very soon a factor that I could not leave our yard unless somebody was with me because they were always afraid of what could happen. And that's why the weekends with my father, walking into the woods and walking along the Rhine, were such big treats.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kinds of things happened-- restrictions, that kind of stuff-- that you can recall from that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing that sticks in my mind very vividly is we had a neighbor directly across the street from our living room. And when there was a specific headline in which was a Nazi newspaper, she would open her window, and sit in the window with the newspaper open so we could see. And she would sit there for hours, holding this newspaper to make sure that we saw what was in This would be maybe once a week, once a month. Whenever it was, whenever a big event happened, that was what would happen. We would see in We could also read-- listen to it on the radio. But she would make sure that we were aware of it. The other thing we knew is that she was always watching our comings and goings. And it became more and more dangerous for people, non-Jews, to come visit us because she would report everybody. She was the eye in the sky, as you might say. And she was always there, always watching. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as far as other things were, pretty soon-- probably 1938-- the last kosher butcher shop closed. They emigrated. And my grandmother wouldn't eat non-kosher meat. So we started having to have kosher meat shipped from Holland because there were no kosher butcher shops in Mannheim, or Ludwigshafen, or Heidelberg anymore, which were the nearest towns. And another thing that happened was that no more matzahs were being made in Germany. And I think it was the Passover of 1938-- '39 for sure, but '38, I think, already-- when we had to get matzahs that were made in Poland. And they were imported. And we had to special order them through our synagogue. And then they would come to us in big boxes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Things became harder and harder. Also, there were demands that my father and my grandfather give up their business. And they resisted for a time. And then, in 1938, they finally had to sell the business to some Germans. And they didn't get much for it. But we had the OK to remain in the house because we owned the house. They only owned the business. So my grandfather still had control over the house, and they were getting some interest from rent. As far as [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things that happened, we-- I think the happiest times were on weekends, when my other uncle, my uncle Ernst, would drive to Mannheim or Heidelberg for an afternoon and evening-- maybe a play for the children, maybe just going up to the castle in Heidelberg, to see something a little bit different. And even then, I remember in Heidelberg, one of the favorite kaffee houses, where we would go in the afternoon for some cake, all of a sudden had a sign on the door, Jews not wanted. And of course, you didn't try to go past that, even if you thought you might be able to pass. It wasn't worth it. And the same signs started appearing in Speyer at certain stores also. So you-- it became more and more restrictive as to what you could and what you couldn't do. The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] population also became more, I would say, antagonistic. They would either ignore you completely or they would just say, Jew, don't come here anymore. Or, if they were-- we were old customers, they would even-- and some of them did that at great risk-- say, don't come here anymore. We'll deliver to you. And then in the night, the doorbell would ring. And they would deliver, so nobody could see, things like hard goods. As far as [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] foods, we were able to get. The grocer and the baker-- there were two bakeries within walking distance. And there was a grocery store at the corner. And we could go there. And we had no problem getting foodstuffs. But other things, we would have to go to Mannheim sometimes or to Heidelberg in order to pick up clothes and stuff like that. In the meanwhile, just about this time, my mother started to have clothing made for us-- for herself, for my father, and me-- for immigration, because they were thinking about it. And everything that she had made had a lot of extra material in it. And this is part of where I say my mother was a very smart woman. Some of these clothes I wore until I came to the United States in 1946, because she had so much extra material in the waist and in the length that they could just keep letting out. And she had clothes made for herself that would do double or triple duty-- skirts with jackets and vests so she could wear it formally or informally, wherever she was. And my-- my father had some extra suits made. And there too, they were of material that was very durable and neutral colors, so that they could work in any environment. And this was '38 and in '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where were they planning to immigrate to?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. My mother had a sister who already lived in the United States, who immigrated in 1937. And at the time, they were called crazy for leaving and selling their business. The whole family was in an uproar. They're crazy. They don't know what they're doing. They're leaving a good business for a country where there is a recession-- depression, in fact. But they left. And they were the only smart ones. Then, all of a sudden, it became, we have to find someone who can get us an affidavit to come to the United States. Since we had no other relatives and these-- my aunt and uncle were struggling-- they couldn't provide for us-- and they started looking for other relatives. And in the meanwhile, also, my father had a sister and a brother at that time already in Israel-- Palestine. And the possibility of going there was also being explored. But my parents wanted the United States. They did not-- my father didn't think he would tolerate the climate in Israel. And we followed this through. And finally, my uncle and aunt found a distant, distant third cousin in Kentucky who was willing to give papers. And she started the ball rolling on getting us papers. And in 1938-- no, 1938, they-- the papers came, late in the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We--

speaker: interviewee
text: But--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now and go to the next--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --tape-- continue this.
